steps.prompt: Which word is the main finite verb?

hints.servire: 'Servire' is an infinitive meaning 'to serve' — it depends on 'potest'

hints.to place: 'To place' is 'ponere' — 'potest' comes from 'posse' (to be able)
hints.to drink: 'To drink' is 'potare' — similar sounding but 'posse' means 'to be able'

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'possit' — 'potest' is indicative

hints.pono, ponere, posui, positum: These are the parts of 'ponere' (to place/put)
hints.poto, potare, potavi, potatum: These are the parts of 'potare' (to drink)
hints.postulo, postulare, postulavi, postulatu: These are the parts of 'postulare' (to demand)

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'domini' — 'dominis' is dative/ablative plural
hints.ablative: 'Dominis' could be ablative, but 'servire' takes a dative object
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'dominorum' — 'dominis' is dative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'domini' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'domini' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'domini' ends in -i

hints.imperative: Imperative would be 'servi' (sg.) or 'servite' (pl.) — 'servire' ends in -re
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'serviat' — 'servire' is infinitive
hints.perfect infinitive: Perfect infinitive would be 'servivisse' — 'servire' is present

hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'serve' — 'servus' is nominative here

translation: No servant can serve two masters
